paper.title: Fast-moving stars around an intermediate-mass black hole in Omega Centauri

extraction.summary: The paper reports seven fast-moving stars in the center of Omega Centauri with velocities above the cluster escape velocity. However, the paper explicitly interprets these stars as bound to an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH), not as Galactic-unbound HVS candidates. The authors rule out ejection scenarios and state the stars' presence 'can only be explained by being bound to a massive black hole.' No objects are discussed as possibly escaping the Milky Way or being unbound from the Galactic potential.

candidate_groups_considered.description: Seven fast-moving stars (A-G) in the central 3 arcseconds of Omega Centauri with 2D proper motions above the cluster escape velocity of 62 km/s.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: The paper explicitly interprets these stars as bound to an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH), analogous to S-stars in the Galactic center. The authors rule out unbound/ejection scenarios (three-body interactions, IMBH ejections) and state their presence 'can only be explained by being bound to a massive black hole.' They are not presented as possibly unbound from the Milky Way Galaxy.

source_refs.context: Paper states fast-moving stars' presence can only be explained by being bound to a massive black hole, and rules out previous debates about lack of fast-moving stars above escape velocity.

Paper explicitly states fast stars are a predicted consequence of an IMBH, not expected from mass-segregated stellar-mass black holes, and would leave the central region in less than 1,000 years if no massive object was present.

Paper rules out alternative scenarios where stars are unbound from the cluster via three-body interactions or IMBH ejections, based on required ejection rates and absence of fast-moving stars at larger radii.
